persona.name: Masahiro Ito

occupation.title: Manufacturing Division General Manager
occupation.organization: Tokai Seiki Co., Ltd., a mid-tier automotive parts manufacturer
occupation.description: Oversees three factory production lines and a staff of 120. His days split between factory floor walk-throughs, quality review meetings, and management meetings with the executive board. Still prefers face-to-face communication but has been forced into Teams calls since COVID. Manages a budget of several hundred million yen annually.

persona.education: B.Eng. in Mechanical Engineering from Nagoya Institute of Technology. Joined Tokai Seiki straight out of university and has been there 33 years. Completed the company's internal management development program at age 40.
long_term_goals: Guide the division through the EV transition without major layoffs
Retire at 63 with enough savings to renovate the family home in rural Mie
Pass on his manufacturing knowledge to younger managers before retiring
Spend more time fishing at Lake Biwa
persona.style: Speaks slowly and deliberately, choosing words carefully in the manner of senior manufacturing managers. Wears a company polo and slacks on the factory floor, switches to a dark suit for board meetings. Prefers whiteboard sketches over PowerPoint. Gives praise sparingly but means it when he does. His emails are short — three lines maximum — and he signs off with his surname only. In social settings he becomes warmer, especially after a beer or two.

traits: Deeply cautious about change — needs proof before commitment
Values face-to-face relationships and in-person trust-building
Paternalistic toward younger staff in a protective way
Stubborn about processes that have worked for decades
Skeptical of buzzwords like 'AI' and 'DX' but listens if shown concrete numbers
Quietly proud of his factory's defect rate being best in the group

interests: Bass fishing at reservoirs around the Tokai region
Japanese history, especially the Sengoku period
Growing vegetables in a small garden plot
Reading the Nikkei morning edition cover to cover
likes: Simple tools that don't require training
Factory floors where machines hum predictably
Miso katsu at the local teishoku-ya
When plans come together without last-minute surprises
dislikes: Software that changes its interface with every update
Young people who stare at laptops during in-person meetings
Being told to 'go digital' by head office staff who've never been on a factory floor
Cloud services where data leaves Japan

beliefs: The best meeting is a 15-minute standing meeting at the gemba
Trust is built through years of working together, not through software
New technology should prove itself on the factory floor before the office adopts it
A manager's job is to remove obstacles, not create new processes
skills: Production line optimization and quality management
Mentoring junior engineers through hands-on teaching

general: Walks the factory floor every morning before any meetings
Takes notes on a small pocket notebook he's carried for 20 years
Asks his secretary to print out meeting agendas and materials
Refuses to install apps on his personal phone for work

morning: Wakes at 5:30, reads the Nikkei with green tea
Checks the garden briefly if weather permits
workday: Morning meeting with line supervisors at 8:15
Quality review and production planning meetings until lunch
Afternoon: management meetings, budget reviews, visitor receptions
Leaves by 18:30 unless there's a quality issue
evening: Dinner at home — wife's cooking, usually fish and rice
Reads a chapter of a historical novel
weekend: Saturday: fishing at a nearby reservoir if weather is good
Sunday: occasionally plays golf with supplier contacts
Watches the Dragons game on TV in the afternoon

health.physical: Slightly overweight, controlled type 2 diabetes managed with diet and medication. Knees starting to ache from years of factory floor walking. Gets the annual company health screening — always dreads the metabolic syndrome check.
health.mental: Steady temperament forged by decades of crisis management on the production line. Worries quietly about the EV transition making his expertise obsolete. Sleeps well but occasionally lies awake thinking about staffing for the next fiscal year.

relationships.name: Fumiko Ito
relationships.description: 54, part-time calligraphy instructor. They've been married 30 years. She handles all household finances and gently pushes him to exercise more. She's better with smartphones than he is.

relationships.name: Takuya Ito
relationships.description: 27, works at a tech startup in Tokyo. Keeps suggesting his father try AI tools, which Masahiro politely deflects. They see each other at Obon and New Year.

relationships.name: Hiroshi Kondo
relationships.relation: Factory floor veteran and trusted advisor
relationships.description: 62, senior technician who's been at the company even longer than Masahiro. They have an unspoken understanding about what the factory needs.
